title: The Macro Minute: Will a Ukraine-Russia peace deal boost markets?

happy Tuesday out there team 42 it's
your Skipper here at Darius Dell to
present our macro minute for Tuesday
February 18th 2025 as always we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
leadoff morning note if you would like
to review the analysis supporting these
conclusions as well as what specific
changes you need to make in your
portfolio to remain on the right side of
Market risk you need to be a client of
42 macro so today's key macro question
is will a Ukraine Russia peace deal
boost markets our answers are a to some
degree they already have by contributing
to strength in a in the US dollar cross
which is positive for Global liquidity
via declining US dollar any deal would
likely require EU officials
demonstrating a collective political
will that has long escaped the common
currency Union while it is impossible to
know how many big Figures were priced
out of the Euro US dollar cross over the
years due to structural resistance to
deeper geopolitical and economic
integration the euro is likely headed
higher if the EU gets his act together
president Trump has ruled out deploying
US troops as part of any Security
package for Ukraine that puts a
tremendous amount of pressure on the EU
to step up its own defense spending
blomberg economics estimates the total
cost of the EU stepping up its military
commitments is $3 trillion doll over 10
years 2.7 of that will come from the EU
members pushing their aggregate defense
budget to 3.5% of GDP which is in line
with the latest NATO discussions
rebuilding Ukraine will cost 234 billion
over 10 years rebuilding Ukraine's
military will cost another 175 billion
over 10 years and the permanent
deployment of peacekeeping troops would
cost another 30 billion over 10 years
all told investors must be prepared to
price in a positive Global demand shock
stemming from increased defense
commitments and Associated fiscal
spending by EU countries that outcome
represents a lasting Tailwind for the
Euro Global defense stocks and Global
bond yields transition to our 42 micro
dashboard here as always a wrap up with
the question from our community uh this
one's regarding Fort Knox audit
implications uh says uh some senator and
Elon Musk are requesting an audit of
Fort Knox gold what would be the
implication of markets of them finding
more or less than what is expected uh
personally I don't believe it's a good
idea to strategically reveal our gold
reserves to our adversaries uh so I mean
one let let me start this is a really
loaded discussion here so I don't want
to waste too much time so let me start
by saying uh there's a lot of um
discussion out there in the you know
Twitter podcast sphere finan you know
talking head podcast sphere about uh
strategic uh um or sorry The Sovereign
wealth fund for the US let me remind you
guys that we don't have any wealth we
are a twin deficit Nation with twin a
current account deficit of somewhere
between two to 5% of GDP pick your year
and a sovereign fiscal balance uh
deficit budget balance deficit that is
somewhere between five to eight% of GDP
in any given year we don't have any
money to acquire wealth we would
literally have to issue debt to acquire
wealth now they're talking about
potentially revaluing our gold reserves
I want to say we have about 8,000 tons
of gold valued currently um at 11
billion based on the 1973 price from the
uh exchange for stabilization fund uh
but if they revalued it to the current
prices it' be somewhere around you know
750 7800 billion dollar so they would
net basically somewhere around $750
billion let me remind everyone that $750
billion sounds like a lot of money but
it's a literal drop at the bucket
relative to our roughly $30 trillion of
outstanding marketable treasury
Securities so uh you know this is all I
hate to say smoking mirrors but it feels
like smoking mirrors I mean there just
is you know anyone who's smart enough to
do basic arithmetic can figure out that
this is not a real serious you know step
in the right direction as it relates to
riging in uh the US's unsustainable
fiscal situation and so going back to
this uh this thing with respect to the
markets what would happen if they
audited for gold and it turns out we had
less gold than what we thought we did
that'd be great for markets because it
ultimately means the dollar is going to
go down uh if they think we're backing
the dollar with eight uh uh um you know
thousand tons of gold and obviously our
military which we're spending you know
just shy of a trillion dollars annually
on uh then you know those two things are
a little bit less or at least one of
those two things is is less um shiny and
Rosy and and less um less of a durable
bull case for the dollar than it would
be uh in in a non- audit scenario and
then if it turns out that we've audited
more then the opposite is true right you
have the US dollar has a a little bit
more strong standing with respect to uh
what it's being backed by but in my
opinion I think what the at the end of
the day what's really backing the US
dollar is not our gold Holdings or it's
really just the you know the Full Faith
and Credit in the United States which
itself is backed by the United States
military with the world's biggest most
capable military nobody wants to F with
us you know for lack of a better word uh
and and they're smart for doing that
right you know we we live in a world
that is increasingly fractured and and
and and um and from a geopolitical
standpoint you know we're moving into a
multi-polar world where we have to take
seriously the geopolitical and strategic
you know goals of China and Russia
relative to our own but it may just be
the case that you know president Trump
which you know some argue he's an
isolationist president it's really not
isolationist he is just an America first
president and an America first president
is really realistically less
confrontational to our adversaries in
China and Russia and so that ultimately
kind of leaves the EU out the dry in
terms of having to kind of pick a side
here and they may choose to pick their
own side as we talked about in our
executive summary just now picking their
own side will require a lot more defense
spending and ultimately would represent
a positive shock to u to to Global
demand from the perspective of increased
defense spending and everything
associated with that so uh we are living
through a you know geopolitically
interesting fractured time it's going to
continue to be inflationary in fact you
you know you look at a lot of folks are
talking about disinflation process in
the United States and whether or not the
FED is on hold but when you look at
inflation statistics from every major
country in the world we're starting to
see a bottoming process across the
entire world in inflation statistics so
um you know I think uh the the
conversation the very narrow myopic
tration esque conversation on Twitter
about US inflation misses the broader
point that uh the rest of the world is
also experiencing a second round
influences on inflation that I think
will be more um obvious as we move
forward in time let's say one to two
quarters so uh I think I've gone on
enough here so uh this is a really fun
topic but at the end of the day we got
to keep everything in context $750
billion dollar of profits revaluing our
gold is a drop in the bucket relative to
our just sh$ 30 trillion doll of
marketable treasury debt we need very
serious credible solutions to address
that and put us on a more sustainable
durable fiscal path and in our opinion
doge is not that solution because
they've rein fenced two-thirds of the
budget essentially from from from those
cuts so we'll see we'll wrap it up there
